title: The Last Independent Bookshop on the Street
summary: An Edinburgh narrator describes how a single independent bookshop has outlived every chain on its street, and what that survival has actually cost the owner.
body: On Victoria Street, in the lower half of Edinburgh's Old Town, there used to be four bookshops. Three of them have closed since the pandemic, and the fourth — Tilly's Books — has somehow survived. Tilly herself bought the shop in 2009, when most people warned her it was the worst possible time. The financial crisis had just hit, and online retailers were taking a larger share of the market every year. If she had listened to that advice, the street would now have no bookshop at all. Instead, she signed a fifteen-year lease and started carrying titles the chains would not touch. She has been running the shop on narrow margins ever since. The strange thing is that her shop has not just survived; it has been growing slowly, even as larger competitors have disappeared. Customers say they come because Tilly remembers what they read last month and recommends something they would never have picked up themselves. She has built a small reading group that meets every Thursday evening, and a handful of local authors have launched their first books from her front window. None of this would have happened if the shop had been run as a chain. But survival has had a personal cost. Tilly has not taken a proper holiday in six years. Her staff has shrunk from three people to one part-time assistant, and she has been told by her landlord that the rent will increase sharply when the lease comes up for renewal next spring. If the increase is as steep as expected, she will have to decide whether to move to a cheaper street, fight the rise in court, or close the shop she has spent a decade building. Locals have started a small campaign asking the council to protect independent businesses, but campaigns like this rarely succeed. Tilly says she is grateful for the support, but she has been around long enough to know that goodwill does not pay rent. What this story shows is not that independent bookshops are dying — many of them are doing surprisingly well. It is that the people who run them are working far harder than most of their customers realise, and that the survival of a small business often depends on one person refusing to give up. If Tilly closes next year, Victoria Street will lose more than a shop. It will lose the only place where strangers still meet to argue about books on a wet Thursday evening.

annotations.meaningVi: thời điểm tồi tệ nhất có thể
annotations.exampleEn: He opened his restaurant during the lockdown — the worst possible time to launch a business.

annotations.meaningVi: các nhà bán lẻ trực tuyến
annotations.exampleEn: Online retailers now account for almost a third of all book sales in the UK.

annotations.meaningVi: biên lợi nhuận mỏng, lãi ít
annotations.exampleEn: Independent shops survive on narrow margins, often making only a few pence per item sold.

annotations.meaningVi: (sách) mà các chuỗi cửa hàng không thèm bán
annotations.exampleEn: She stocks small-press poetry that the chains would not touch.

annotations.meaningVi: tủ kính phía trước, cửa sổ trưng bày
annotations.exampleEn: Local authors launched their first novels from the shop's front window.

annotations.meaningVi: cái giá phải trả về mặt cá nhân
annotations.exampleEn: Keeping the business open has had a real personal cost for the owner.
Her team has shrunk from three to one over the last two years.
My contract comes up for renewal in March, so I'll have to negotiate then.

annotations.meaningVi: cao như dự đoán, dốc như dự đoán
annotations.exampleEn: If the price increase is as steep as expected, many tenants will move out.

annotations.meaningVi: kiện ra tòa để chống lại việc tăng giá
annotations.exampleEn: Some tenants choose to fight the rise in court rather than accept it.
The council has the power to protect certain shops as heritage businesses.

annotations.meaningVi: thiện chí (của khách hàng) không trả được tiền thuê nhà
annotations.exampleEn: She said she appreciated the kind messages, but goodwill does not pay rent.
The shop has survived because of one person refusing to give up.

annotations.meaningVi: đang hoạt động tốt một cách đáng ngạc nhiên
annotations.exampleEn: Some independent bookshops are doing surprisingly well, despite the doom and gloom.
There are still places where strangers meet to argue about books.

annotations.meaningVi: một tối thứ Năm mưa ướt
annotations.exampleEn: Even on a wet Thursday evening, the reading group is always full.

grammarNotes.title: Third Conditional vs Second Conditional
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Đoạn văn dùng câu điều kiện loại 3 cho tình huống đã xảy ra trong quá khứ: 'If she had listened to that advice, the street would now have no bookshop at all' (cô ấy đã không nghe, kết quả là phố vẫn còn hiệu sách). Đồng thời cũng dùng loại 1/2 cho khả năng tương lai: 'If the increase is as steep as expected, she will have to decide...' (loại 1 — khả năng thực tế). Quy tắc: loại 3 = 'if + had + V3, would have + V3' cho quá khứ giả định; loại 1 = 'if + present, will + verb' cho tương lai có thực.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The passage uses third conditional for a counterfactual past: 'If she had listened to that advice, the street would now have no bookshop at all' (she did not listen — the result is a hypothetical past). It also uses first conditional for a real future possibility: 'If the increase is as steep as expected, she will have to decide...' Rule: third conditional = 'if + had + past participle, would have + past participle' for hypothetical past; first conditional = 'if + present, will + verb' for likely future.

grammarNotes.title: Present Perfect Continuous for Ongoing Effort
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Đoạn văn dùng thì hiện tại hoàn thành tiếp diễn để nhấn mạnh hoạt động kéo dài và vẫn đang tiếp tục: 'She has been running the shop on narrow margins ever since', 'it has been growing slowly', 'Tilly has not taken a proper holiday in six years'. Cấu trúc: have/has + been + V-ing. Dùng khi muốn nhấn mạnh quá trình, nỗ lực hoặc thời gian kéo dài, đặc biệt với các trạng từ như 'ever since', 'for years', 'lately'.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The passage uses the present perfect continuous to stress sustained, ongoing effort: 'She has been running the shop on narrow margins ever since', 'it has been growing slowly', 'Tilly has not taken a proper holiday in six years'. Form: have/has + been + V-ing. Use when emphasising process, effort, or duration — especially with adverbs like 'ever since', 'for years', 'lately'.

translationVi: Trên phố Victoria, ở nửa dưới của khu Phố Cổ Edinburgh, từng có bốn hiệu sách. Ba trong số đó đã đóng cửa kể từ đại dịch, và cái thứ tư — Tilly's Books — bằng cách nào đó vẫn sống sót. Tilly mua hiệu sách vào năm 2009, khi hầu hết mọi người cảnh báo cô rằng đó là thời điểm tồi tệ nhất có thể. Khủng hoảng tài chính vừa nổ ra, và các nhà bán lẻ trực tuyến đang chiếm thị phần lớn hơn mỗi năm. Nếu cô đã nghe theo lời khuyên đó, con phố giờ sẽ không có hiệu sách nào. Thay vào đó, cô ký hợp đồng thuê mười lăm năm và bắt đầu bán những đầu sách mà các chuỗi không thèm bán. Cô đã điều hành hiệu sách với biên lợi nhuận mỏng kể từ đó. Điều kỳ lạ là hiệu sách của cô không chỉ sống sót; nó còn đang phát triển từ từ, ngay cả khi các đối thủ lớn hơn đã biến mất. Khách hàng nói họ đến vì Tilly nhớ tháng trước họ đọc gì và gợi ý những cuốn mà họ sẽ không bao giờ tự chọn. Cô đã lập một nhóm đọc nhỏ gặp nhau mỗi tối thứ Năm, và một số tác giả địa phương đã ra mắt cuốn sách đầu tay từ ô cửa kính phía trước. Tất cả những điều này sẽ không xảy ra nếu hiệu sách được điều hành như một chuỗi. Nhưng việc sống sót đã có cái giá cá nhân. Tilly đã không có một kỳ nghỉ tử tế trong sáu năm. Nhân viên của cô đã giảm từ ba người xuống một trợ lý bán thời gian, và chủ nhà đã thông báo rằng tiền thuê sẽ tăng mạnh khi hợp đồng đến hạn gia hạn vào mùa xuân tới. Nếu mức tăng cao như dự đoán, cô sẽ phải quyết định: chuyển sang một con phố rẻ hơn, kiện ra tòa, hay đóng cửa hiệu sách mà cô đã xây dựng cả thập kỷ. Người dân địa phương đã bắt đầu một chiến dịch nhỏ kêu gọi hội đồng bảo vệ các doanh nghiệp độc lập, nhưng những chiến dịch như vậy hiếm khi thành công. Tilly nói cô biết ơn sự ủng hộ, nhưng cô đã ở trong nghề đủ lâu để biết rằng thiện chí không trả được tiền thuê. Điều câu chuyện này cho thấy không phải là các hiệu sách độc lập đang chết — nhiều hiệu vẫn hoạt động tốt một cách đáng ngạc nhiên. Mà là những người điều hành chúng đang làm việc vất vả hơn nhiều so với những gì hầu hết khách hàng nhận ra, và rằng sự sống còn của một doanh nghiệp nhỏ thường phụ thuộc vào một người không chịu bỏ cuộc. Nếu Tilly đóng cửa năm tới, phố Victoria sẽ mất nhiều hơn là một hiệu sách. Nó sẽ mất nơi duy nhất mà người lạ vẫn gặp nhau để tranh luận về sách vào một tối thứ Năm mưa ướt.

questions.prompt: How many bookshops used to be on Victoria Street?
questions.explanation: The passage states 'On Victoria Street... there used to be four bookshops.'
questions.hint: Listen for the number at the start of the passage.

questions.prompt: When did Tilly buy the shop?
questions.explanation: The passage says 'Tilly herself bought the shop in 2009.'
questions.hint: The year is mentioned alongside the financial crisis.

questions.prompt: Why did people warn Tilly against buying the shop in 2009?
options: The shop had a bad reputation
The financial crisis had hit and online retailers were taking market share
She had no experience in retail
questions.explanation: The passage explains 'The financial crisis had just hit, and online retailers were taking a larger share of the market every year.'
questions.hint: The advice was based on two specific economic conditions.

questions.prompt: How long did Tilly sign her lease for?
questions.explanation: The passage says 'she signed a fifteen-year lease.'
questions.hint: The lease length is mentioned alongside her decision to buy.

questions.prompt: Why do customers say they come to Tilly's shop?
options: Because the prices are lower than online
Because Tilly remembers what they read and makes personal recommendations
Because it has a café attached
Because it is the only shop near a transport hub
questions.explanation: The passage says 'Tilly remembers what they read last month and recommends something they would never have picked up themselves.'
questions.hint: The reason relates to Tilly's personal knowledge of her customers.

questions.prompt: What happens at Tilly's shop every Thursday evening?
questions.explanation: The passage says 'She has built a small reading group that meets every Thursday evening.'
questions.hint: The event involves a regular group of readers.

options: It has grown from one to three
It has shrunk from three people to one part-time assistant
It has remained the same size
It has been replaced entirely by volunteers
questions.explanation: The passage states 'Her staff has shrunk from three people to one part-time assistant.'
questions.hint: The change reflects the financial pressure on the shop.

questions.prompt: What three options will Tilly face if the rent rises sharply?
options: Hire more staff, raise prices, or expand the shop
Move to a cheaper street, fight the rise in court, or close the shop
Sell the building, lease out part of the space, or convert it into a café
Apply for a loan, find a partner, or go online only
questions.explanation: The passage states she will have to decide whether 'to move to a cheaper street, fight the rise in court, or close the shop she has spent a decade building.'
questions.hint: Three specific options are listed together.

questions.prompt: What is Tilly's attitude to the local campaign?
options: She believes it will definitely save her shop
She refuses to be involved with it
She is grateful for the support but knows goodwill does not pay rent
She thinks it makes her customers feel guilty
questions.explanation: The passage says she 'is grateful for the support, but she has been around long enough to know that goodwill does not pay rent.'
questions.hint: Her attitude is a mix of two feelings — appreciation and realism.

questions.prompt: What does the narrator say independent bookshops in general are doing?
options: Doing surprisingly well, though the owners work very hard
questions.explanation: The passage says 'many of them are doing surprisingly well' but the owners 'are working far harder than most of their customers realise.'
questions.hint: The narrator pushes back against the assumption that bookshops are dying.

questions.prompt: According to the narrator, what does the survival of a small business often depend on?
options: One person refusing to give up
Loyal employees willing to work for less
questions.explanation: The passage states that survival 'often depends on one person refusing to give up.'
questions.hint: The narrator identifies a single human factor.

questions.prompt: What would Victoria Street lose, beyond a shop, if Tilly closes?
options: The only place where strangers meet to argue about books on a wet Thursday evening
questions.explanation: The passage ends 'It will lose the only place where strangers still meet to argue about books on a wet Thursday evening.'
questions.hint: The loss is described as social and cultural, not commercial.

questions.prompt: Which statement best captures the overall tone of the passage?
options: Admiring but clear-eyed about the costs
questions.explanation: The narrator praises Tilly's success while honestly describing the personal costs — no holidays, shrinking staff, uncertain future — without sentimentality.
questions.hint: The narrator balances admiration with realism.

cloze.template: On Victoria Street, there used to be four bookshops. Three of them have closed since the pandemic, and the fourth — Tilly's Books — has somehow {{b1}}. Tilly bought the shop in 2009, when most people warned her it was the worst possible time. The financial crisis had just hit, and online {{b2}} were taking a larger share of the market every year. If she had listened to that advice, the street would now have no bookshop at all. Instead, she signed a fifteen-year {{b3}} and started carrying titles the chains would not touch. She has been running the shop on narrow {{b4}} ever since. Customers say they come because Tilly remembers what they read last month and {{b5}} something they would never have picked up themselves. She has built a small reading group that meets every Thursday evening, and a handful of local authors have launched their first books from her front {{b6}}. But survival has had a personal {{b7}}. Tilly has not taken a proper holiday in six years. Her staff has {{b8}} from three people to one part-time assistant, and the rent will increase sharply when the lease comes up for {{b9}} next spring. Locals have started a small campaign asking the {{b10}} to protect independent businesses, but Tilly says goodwill does not pay rent.

blanks.explanation: The passage states the fourth shop 'has somehow survived' — the surrounding sentences make clear it is still open.
'Online retailers' is the exact phrase used — it's the standard term for internet-based sellers.
A 'lease' is the specific legal term for a rental agreement on a property — the passage says 'fifteen-year lease'.
'Narrow margins' is the fixed expression meaning small profit per sale — the standard business phrase.
'Recommends' is the verb used — it implies an active personal endorsement, stronger than 'suggests'.
'Front window' is the exact phrase — the display window of a shop is where book launches are visually staged.
'Personal cost' is the exact phrase used and the most natural collocation in this register.
'Shrunk' is the past participle of 'shrink' — the exact verb used in the passage for staff reduction.
'Comes up for renewal' is the fixed expression for when a lease term ends and must be renewed.
The 'council' is the local authority in UK cities — it has the power to protect heritage businesses.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Tilly says 'goodwill does not pay rent'. If a community truly values an independent shop, what should it do beyond writing supportive messages — and is it fair to expect customers to pay more to keep small businesses alive?
